examples.title: Algorithm: Thread Creation and Synchronization
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the general algorithm for creating threads, passing parameters, and synchronizing using join/wait operations
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ThreadCreation
INPUT: task_parameter
OUTPUT: result

BEGIN
 // Declare thread control structures
 DECLARE thread_id
 DECLARE thread_attributes
 DECLARE shared_data
 DECLARE result

 // Initialize thread attributes (default settings)
 CALL InitializeAttributes(thread_attributes)

 // Create a new thread that executes worker_function
 CALL CreateThread(thread_id, thread_attributes, worker_function, task_parameter)

 // Main thread waits for created thread to complete
 CALL JoinThread(thread_id, result)

 // Clean up thread attributes
 CALL DestroyAttributes(thread_attributes)

 // Output the computed result
 PRINT "Result:", result
END

ALGORITHM worker_function
INPUT: param
OUTPUT: computed_value

BEGIN
 DECLARE local_sum = 0
 DECLARE upper = ConvertToInteger(param)

 // Compute sum from 1 to upper
 FOR i = 1 TO upper DO
 local_sum = local_sum + i
 END FOR

 // Store result in shared memory (requires synchronization in real code)
 SHARED_DATA = local_sum

 // Terminate thread and return value
 RETURN local_sum
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the general pattern for thread creation across different libraries. The main thread creates a worker thread using CreateThread, passes a parameter to it, and uses JoinThread to wait for completion. The worker performs computation and stores results in shared memory.

examples.title: Python: Thread Library Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation using the threading module to sum numbers from 1 to N, demonstrating thread creation, starting, and joining
examples.code:
# Python Thread Library Example - Summing Numbers
# Uses Python's threading module (similar to Pthreads but at user level)

import threading
import sys

# Shared data between threads
sum_result = 0
sum_lock = threading.Lock() # Mutex for synchronization

def compute_sum(upper):
 """Worker function that runs in a separate thread"""
 global sum_result
 local_sum = 0
 
 # Compute sum from 1 to upper
 for i in range(1, upper + 1):
 local_sum += i
 
 # Use lock to safely update shared data
 with sum_lock:
 sum_result = local_sum
 
 print(f"Thread completed: computed sum = {local_sum}")
 return local_sum

def main():
 # Get upper limit from command line
 if len(sys.argv) != 2:
 print("Usage: python thread_sum.py <number>")
 sys.exit(1)
 
 upper = int(sys.argv[1])
 
 print(f"Main: Creating thread to sum 1 to {upper}")
 
 # Create a new thread
 # target specifies the function to run
 # args specifies the arguments to pass
 worker_thread = threading.Thread(
 target=compute_sum, 
 args=(upper,)
 )
 
 # Start the thread (calls compute_sum(upper))
 worker_thread.start()
 
 # Wait for thread to complete (similar to pthread_join)
 worker_thread.join()
 
 # Print the result after thread completes
 print(f"Main: Final sum = {sum_result}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: Python's threading module provides a high-level interface for thread creation. The threading.Thread class is similar to Java's Runnable approach. We use threading.Lock() for mutex synchronization when accessing shared data. The join() method blocks until the thread completes, similar to pthread_join() in C.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the upper limit
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for the computation, O(n) for thread stack

examples.title: C: POSIX Pthreads Implementation
examples.description: C implementation using POSIX Pthreads to create a thread that computes the sum of numbers from 1 to N
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates POSIX Pthreads (pthread_create, pthread_join, pthread_exit). The main thread creates a worker thread that computes the sum. We use pthread_mutex_t for synchronization when updating the shared sum variable. Compile with: gcc -pthread program.c -o program. The -pthread flag is required to link the pthread library.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the input number
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for computation, O(n) for thread stack

examples.title: Java: Thread Creation with Thread Class and Runnable
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating both methods of creating threads: extending Thread class and implementing Runnable interface
examples.explanation: Java provides two ways to create threads: (1) Extending the Thread class - simpler but uses single inheritance, (2) Implementing Runnable interface - more flexible as the class can still extend another class. Both methods use start() to begin execution (not run() directly). The join() method blocks until the thread completes, similar to pthread_join in C. Java's volatile keyword ensures visibility across threads for the completed flag.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the input number
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for computation, O(n) for thread stack
